preamble: The shift from a voiceless labial stop [p] to a voiceless palatal stop [c] is a rare but well-documented form of 'labial palatalization'. This typically occurs when a labial consonant is followed by a high front glide [j] or vowel [i], leading to the loss of labial articulation and the emergence of a palatal stop. This is found in Bantu languages and some dialects of Greek and Southern Italian.

examples.note: Many Bantu languages exhibit palatalization of labials before high front glides (e.g., passive formations).
In some Greek dialects (e.g., Lesbos), /pj/ becomes [cc] or [c], such as 'spitia' > [stica].

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
